one_line_takeaway: By replacing SecAlign's sparse, hand-crafted attacker targets with LLM-generated 'near-but-wrong' targets and a margin-aware DPO reweighting scheme, LocalAlign cuts out-of-distribution prompt-injection attack success rates by roughly 5-10x over SecAlign (e.g., Qasper 45.0%->7.0%, InjecAgent 13.5%->0.0%) while keeping utility within 2 points of the undefended model, though multi-hop-reasoning OOD scenarios (HotpotQA) remain comparatively harder to defend.
key_finding: On i.i.d. data (Llama3.1, Table 2): SecAlign already achieves 1.44% ASR under optimization-free attacks and 0.00% under GCG; LocalAlign matches or slightly improves this (0.00% optimization-free, 0.00% GCG), with the clearest gain under adaptive GCG where LocalAlign reaches 0.48% ASR vs SecAlign's 2.40% (undefended model: 92.31%; StruQ: 11.54%) — an ~80% relative reduction over SecAlign. The larger gains appear OOD (Table 1): on Qasper with Llama3.1, LocalAlign reduces SecAlign's ASR from 45.0% to 7.0% (optimization-free) and from 56.0% to 9.0% (adaptive optimization-free); on InjecAgent with Llama3.1, LocalAlign reduces SecAlign's 13.5%/6.4% ASR to 0.0%/0.0%. On Qwen3, LocalAlign likewise drives ASR toward 0% on most OOD datasets (e.g., MMLU 11.60%->0.0%, Open-Prompt 3.84%->0.0% under optimization-free), though InjecAgent under adaptive optimization-free remains at 15.70% (down from SecAlign's 68.20%). One reported weak spot: HotpotQA with Llama3.1 under adaptive optimization-free attacks only improves from 55.0% (SecAlign) to 23.0% (LocalAlign) — the authors attribute this to HotpotQA's multi-hop reasoning coupling the injected command tightly with task-relevant content. Utility (Table 3) is preserved: LocalAlign never drops more than 2% below SecAlign on any of the 5 utility benchmarks (largest drop: BBH on Qwen3, 2.0 points), and on some benchmarks (e.g., MMLU-Pro on Llama3.1: 44.12% vs SecAlign's 42.24%; BBH on Llama3.1: 71.37% vs 71.03%) LocalAlign exceeds SecAlign. Ablations (Table 4, Llama3.1, OOD, optimization-free): removing warming-up raises HotpotQA ASR from 8.0% to 17.0%; removing near-target generation (reverting to SecAlign-style fixed targets) raises InjecAgent ASR from 0.0% to 21.5%; replacing MAA with standard DPO raises Qasper ASR from 7.0% to 20.0% — each component is shown to contribute independently.
core_contribution: LocalAlign, a fine-tuning-based defense against (indirect) prompt injection that builds on SecAlign's DPO-style preference optimization but replaces SecAlign's fixed, hand-crafted attacker target responses with automatically generated 'near-target' adversarial examples. Given a trusted command x_cmd, untrusted data x_data, and the correct response y_correct produced by a warmed-up model, LocalAlign uses a single LLM inference call with a dedicated generation prompt G to synthesize an injected command x_inj whose direct response y_target is close to y_correct in embedding space (same task/output format) yet non-identical, and inserts x_inj into x_data. The model is then trained with a margin-aware DPO variant (MAA) that computes a per-sample margin m_i = log pi(y_correct|x) - log pi(y_target|x), z-score-normalizes it within each mini-batch, and rescales the DPO coefficient beta_i = beta_0 * exp(lambda * tanh(z_i)) so that samples with smaller (harder) margins get stronger alignment pressure, bounded to beta_i in [beta_0 e^-lambda, beta_0 e^lambda]. The paper also gives a formal argument (Assumptions 1-2, Proposition 1) that training on only a sparse hand-crafted target set Ytrain(x) does not guarantee a positive margin over the full near-target neighborhood N_epsilon(s, y_correct), motivating the near-target generation strategy.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection: input x = (x_cmd, x_data) where x_cmd (system/user command) is trusted and fixed, and x_data (retrieved documents, tool outputs, emails, etc.) is untrusted and attacker-controlled. Attacker's knowledge: white-box access to the model, using both prompt-engineering attacks (direct injection, context ignoring, fake completion, escape-based, combined) and gradient-based optimization (GCG and adaptive/embedding-space GCG variants). Attacker's capability: can fully control x_data but cannot modify x_cmd. Defender is restricted to modifying only the base model's parameters (prevention-based, not detection-based), with zero additional inference-time delay/latency and no external detector modules, and does not know in advance which attack strategy or attacker objective will be used.
limitations: (1) The near-target generation step itself relies on prompting an LLM to produce the injected command/target — the paper does not report the failure rate of this generator (e.g., how often the non-identical or same-task-family constraints are violated) at scale, only that these constraints are 'required.' (2) Residual weakness on HotpotQA (23.0% ASR under adaptive optimization-free attacks even after LocalAlign) explicitly acknowledged by the authors as tied to multi-hop retrieval/reasoning scenarios where the injected command is entangled with legitimate task content. (3) The theoretical result (Proposition 1) is a qualitative existence argument (that sparse-target training does not guarantee robustness over the near-target neighborhood) rather than a quantitative bound relating epsilon, sample count, or generalization error. (4) Evaluated on only two open-weight models (Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-4B-Instruct); no evaluation on larger or closed models. (5) LocalAlign is built on top of / requires an existing fine-tuning defense (SecAlign) for the warming-up stage, so it is an enhancement to, not a replacement for, that pipeline, and inherits SecAlign's DPO training cost plus the added cost of generation (though the paper argues this remains far cheaper than input-side GCG-style adversarial training).

datasets_used: MMLU (OOD security eval and utility eval)
Open-Prompt (OOD, from Liu et al. 2024 formalized prompt injection benchmark)
quantitative_results: Llama3.1 i.i.d. (Table 2): GCG ASR — None 26.92%, StruQ 16.35%, SecAlign 0.00%, LocalAlign 0.00%
Llama3.1 i.i.d. (Table 2): Adaptive GCG ASR — None 92.31%, StruQ 11.54%, SecAlign 2.40%, LocalAlign 0.48%
Llama3.1 OOD Qasper (Table 1): optimization-free ASR SecAlign 45.0% -> LocalAlign 7.0%; adaptive optimization-free 56.0% -> 9.0%
Llama3.1 OOD InjecAgent (Table 1): optimization-free ASR SecAlign 13.5% -> LocalAlign 0.0%; adaptive optimization-free 6.4% -> 0.0%
Llama3.1 OOD HotpotQA (Table 1): adaptive optimization-free ASR SecAlign 55.0% -> LocalAlign 23.0%
Qwen3 OOD InjecAgent (Table 1): optimization-free ASR SecAlign 43.50% -> LocalAlign 4.60%
Utility (Table 3), Llama3.1 MMLU-Pro: None 42.25%, SecAlign 42.24%, LocalAlign 44.12%
Utility (Table 3), Llama3.1 BBH: None 71.63%, SecAlign 71.03%, LocalAlign 71.37%
Utility (Table 3), Qwen3 BBH: None 76.64%, SecAlign 77.66%, LocalAlign 75.66% (largest LocalAlign utility drop vs SecAlign, 2.0 points)
Ablation (Table 4), Llama3.1 OOD optimization-free: w/o warming-up HotpotQA ASR 17.0% vs full LocalAlign 8.0%
Ablation (Table 4): w/o near-target generation InjecAgent ASR 21.5% vs full LocalAlign 0.0%
Ablation (Table 4): w/o margin-aware alignment (standard DPO) Qasper ASR 20.0% vs full LocalAlign 7.0%

Estimated training cost: standard fine-tuning ~2.5 GPU-hours-equivalent (2x H100, 2.5 hours) vs ~31,928 GPU-hours estimated for direct GCG-style input-side adversarial training (>4 orders of magnitude higher)

Correlation supporting near-target hypothesis (Figure 2, AlpacaFarm under SecAlign, N=6133 filtered samples): Pearson r = -0.2238 between embedding cosine distance (correct vs attacker-target response) and attacker-target sequence log-probability
baselines_compared: SecAlign / SecAlign++ (DPO-style preference-optimization defense; state-of-the-art fine-tuning baseline that LocalAlign builds directly on top of)
